mnemonics.title: Duck Typing Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: DUCK: Don't Use Concrete Knowledge - Just check if the object can do what you need (has required methods)

mnemonics.title: Abstract ABC
mnemonics.content: ABC: Abstract Base Class - Can't instantiate, needs implementation from child

acronyms.usage: Base class in Python that cannot be instantiated; enforces method implementation in subclasses using @abstractmethod decorator
Defines the sequence in which Python searches for methods in a class hierarchy, viewable via ClassName.mro()
Paradigm using objects; four pillars: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction
Special methods like __add__, __str__ that enable operator overloading and customization of built-in behaviors

visualTips.visualization: Draw a family tree: Parent method (base class) → Child overrides it (subclass). The child version is used when object is of child type, regardless of reference type.
Think of a duck pond: If something walks and quacks like a duck, treat it as a duck regardless of whether it's actually a duck (could be a toy duck, robot duck, etc.)
Single arrow → splits into multiple paths (Circle, Rectangle, Triangle) → each takes different action but called by same method name area()

analogies.analogy: Remote control buttons - same 'volume up' button works on TV, AC, or music player, each responding differently based on the device.
Restaurant menu - the menu (abstract class) lists 'Pizza' (abstract method), but actual pizza recipe varies by chef (subclass implementation).
+ operator is like a universal translator - with numbers it adds, with strings it concatenates, with lists it extends. Operator overloading lets custom classes speak this same language.
ATM machine - doesn't ask 'are you a human?' It just needs someone with a card and PIN. If they can perform the required actions, it works regardless of who they are.
